articles.author: Paul Wiseman
articles.description: U.S. consumer prices climbed a sharply again last month as the 10-week war with Iran pushed energy prices higher. The Labor Department reported Tuesday that its consumer price index rose 3.8% from April 2025. On a month-to-month basis, April prices were up 0.6% from March as gasoline prices rose 5.4%. Excluding volatile food and energy costs, so-called consumer core prices were up 0.4% last month from March and 2.8% from April 2025, relatively modest readings that suggest the energy price burst isn’t yet spilling over much into other prices.
articles.title: US consumer prices rise 3.8% as Iran war sends energy prices higher

articles.author: Bridget Brown
articles.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will face a new round of questioning from lawmakers over the Iran war Tuesday, including some Republicans who have expressed concerns over the war.
articles.title: Live updates: Hegseth will face a new round of questioning from Congress on the Iran war

articles.description: When the cost of nearly everything is rising, economists call it inflation. Even if paychecks also rise, inflation typically leads people feel like it's harder to make ends meet, and it often becomes a major political issue
articles.title: Why is everything so expensive? Business basics: Inflation

articles.author: Anton L. Delgado, Chan Ho-him
articles.description: The global maritime industry is facing a looming shortage of the fuel that powers ships as the Iran war chokes off a key shipping lane in the Strait of Hormuz. About 80% of globally traded goods are transported by sea. Ship operators rely on a sludgelike substance known as bunker fuel to move those goods. The world’s largest ship refueling hub is Southeast Asia, which places the region at the center of growing concerns as prices skyrocket. Experts say consumers and companies should brace for price spikes to shipping costs and impacts to bottom lines. Interest in “green” fuels could grow.
articles.title: Iran war drives shipping fuel shortage for global maritime industry

articles.author: Margery A. Beck, Steve Peoples
articles.description: The fate of Nebraska’s “blue dot” will play prominently as Democratic voters select a congressional nominee in the state’s 2nd District. The Omaha-area district, where Republican U.S. Rep. Don Bacon is retiring, is one of Democrats' biggest targets this year. It’s also a national focus every four years because Nebraska is one of just two states that splits its electoral votes in presidential elections. The 2nd District has gone to Democratic presidential candidates three times since 2008 — creating a “blue dot” in an otherwise sea of red. Three Democrats are seen as the top contenders in Tuesday's congressional primary: state Sen. John Cavanaugh, political activist Denise Powell and district court clerk Crystal Rhoades.
articles.title: Nebraska's 'blue dot' takes center stage in Democratic House primary

articles.author: David A. Lieb, Kim Chandler, Mark Sherman
articles.description: The U.S. Supreme Court has set the stage for Alabama to get rid of one of two largely Black congressional districts before this year’s midterm elections. The decision Monday comes after the court in April struck down a majority-Black U.S. House district in Louisiana as an illegal racial gerrymander. Alabama has been under a court order to use two largely Black districts but asked the court to reconsider that in light of the Louisiana ruling. The high court overturned that order and directed a lower court to reconsider the case. Alabama wants to instead use a map passed in 2023 by the Republican-led legislature that has one majority-Black district.
articles.title: Supreme Court halts Alabama order for 2 largely Black US House districts

articles.author: David A. Lieb, Jeffrey Collins, Jack Brook
articles.description: Missouri's new congressional districts are facing a key legal challenge while Republican lawmakers in Louisiana and South Carolina consider redistricting. The Missouri Supreme Court is to hear arguments Tuesday on two cases. One contends the new map backed by President Donald Trump should be tossed out because the districts are not compact. The other contends the new districts should have been suspended when opponents submitted petition signatures seeking a referendum. Legislative committees in Louisiana and South Carolina both are to consider redistricting plans Tuesday that could help Republicans win additional seats.
articles.title: Trump's redistricting push faces legal and political challenges

articles.author: Jennifer Sinco Kelleher, Geoff Mulvihill
articles.description: Two states could try to squeeze companies' money out of politics by redefining the powers of corporations. Advocates see the effort as a way to get around the Supreme Court's Citizens United decision that opened the door to more outside election spending. Lawmakers in Hawaii have passed a measure that's headed to the governor and a similar measure could go before voters this year in Montana. The idea is to redefine the state-granted powers of corporations to curtail political spending by companies and “dark money” groups that aren't required to disclose their donors. Some legal scholars say courts would likely reject this approach, too.
articles.title: States explore how to limit corporate political money

articles.author: Aamer Madhani
articles.description: President Donald Trump is set to leave for Beijing for high-stakes talks with President Xi Jinping. The U.S. has been trying to persuade China to use its influence over Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. China, a major buyer of Iranian oil, insists it wants the war to end even as the U.S. accuses Chinese firms of aiding Iran's military. The White House has set low expectations for Trump's visit to change China's stance. Instead, the focus is on keep trade relations stable. Both nations want to avoid further straining their relationship and risk further roiling global markets.
articles.title: Trump heads to Beijing for talks with Xi as Iran war looms

articles.author: Josh Boak, Didi Tang
articles.description: President Donald Trump claims America is profiting from trade with China despite tensions over rare earth minerals, tariffs and emerging technologies like AI. Trump departs Tuesday for a summit in Beijing with Chinese leader Xi Jinping. It will aim to keep the economic relationship stable, with only modest policy announcements expected. The trade truce reached last October will likely be extended, and China may announce plans to buy American soybeans, beef and Boeing airplanes. The U.S. also wants a new Board of Trade to guide trade with China. Despite Trump’s claims about making money, China bought nearly $50 billion less in American products last year than in 2022.
articles.title: Trump aims to stabilize trade in Beijing summit with Xi
